steps.title: Cardiology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Secondary Care

steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: What is the correct dosage of intramuscular benzathine penicillin for a child weighing over 25 kg with ARF? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Children >25 kg should receive 1,200,000 IU IM as a stat dose. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: What is the recommended first-line treatment for joint pain in ARF? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg TDS for 7 days (or until joint symptoms resolve) is the guideline-recommended anti-inflammatory treatment. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg three times a day for 7 days

steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate treatment for severe or distressing chorea in ARF? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Haloperidol or sodium valproate are used to treat severe chorea. Mild chorea may not require treatment. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: In cases of severe chorea, which medication can be added to haloperidol or valproate? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Prednisone (2 mg/kg daily) can be added in severe or distressing cases of chorea, then tapered. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Acute rheumatic fever & rheumatic heart disease
steps.text: What is the minimum recommended duration of prophylactic benzathine penicillin in mild cases of rheumatic heart disease? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Mild cases should receive secondary prophylaxis until 21 years of age; moderate to severe cases require life-long prophylaxis. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Myocarditis
steps.text: Which of the following best describes the appropriate treatment at secondary and tertiary care levels for a child with suspected myocarditis? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Treatment at secondary and tertiary levels includes: stabilization, heart failure management if symptoms are present, and antibiotics if there is clinical evidence of bacterial infection. Tertiary care adds cardiology review, but the core treatment overlaps. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Monitor for 72 hours and refer only if symptoms worsen
Stabilize the patient, treat as heart failure if symptomatic, and start antibiotics if bacterial infection is suspected
Begin antiviral therapy and schedule cardiology review later

steps.title: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
steps.text: What is the appropriate management of a child diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy at the secondary or tertiary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes stabilizing the patient and managing them as heart failure if symptoms are present. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Begin corticosteroids and reassess in one week
Stabilize the patient and treat as heart failure if symptoms are present
Delay treatment until echocardiogram confirms diagnosis
Start antibiotics routinely for all cases

steps.title: Pulmonary hypertension
steps.text: A child with pulmonary hypertension presents with dyspnoea and mild leg swelling. What combination of interventions is most appropriate at the secondary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Key secondary-level management includes supplemental oxygen, heart failure management if symptoms are present, and treating any reversible underlying causes such as lung disease or congenital heart defects [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Begin sildenafil, restrict fluids, and refer immediately
Administer corticosteroids, discharge with oxygen, and encourage walking
Provide supplemental oxygen, manage heart failure symptoms, and assess for treatable underlying causes
Delay treatment and monitor for signs of right heart failure before intervening

steps.title: Congenital Heart diseases
steps.text: A child with suspected CHD is admitted at secondary level. They are tachypnoeic with hepatomegaly. What is the most appropriate initial treatment? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Heart failure treatment at this level includes furosemide and spironolactone if symptoms are present, along with stabilization. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Hypercyanotic Spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
steps.text: A child with Tetralogy of Fallot presents with a hypercyanotic spell. Which medication should be given at the secondary care level to reduce distress and respiratory drive? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: PO morphine helps reduce hyperpnoea and distress, both of which exacerbate cyanosis during a Tet spell. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Hypercyanotic Spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
steps.text: Which of the following is part of the ongoing medical management for children with Tet spells at the secondary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Propranolol helps relieve right ventricular outflow tract obstruction by reducing infundibular spasm, and is used as part of ongoing medical management. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Congestive Heart Failure
steps.text: A 6-month-old infant with CHF is admitted to a secondary facility. What combination of medications is recommended as part of anti-failure treatment? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends furosemide (1–2 mg/kg IV BD or TDS) and spironolactone (1–2 mg/kg PO BD) as the standard anti-failure regimen. [cite: 1]

steps.title: Congestive Heart Failure
steps.text: What is the purpose of placing a child in a propped-up position during management of congestive heart failure? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The propped-up position helps ease breathing and improve lung expansion in children with heart failure-related pulmonary congestion. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: To improve respiratory effort and reduce pulmonary congestion

steps.title: Congestive Heart Failure
steps.text: What is an appropriate nutritional strategy for an infant with CHF being managed at the secondary level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline emphasizes nutritional support with optimized caloric intake and a low threshold for nasogastric feeding due to high metabolic demand and poor oral intake. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Delay feeding until respiratory symptoms improve
Optimize caloric intake and consider nasogastric feeding if needed
Use only IV fluids until referral

steps.title: Paediatric Arrhythimias
steps.text: A 7-year-old presents to a primary care facility with altered consciousness and a heart rate over 220 bpm. The child appears pale, cold, and poorly perfused. What is the most appropriate action? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: This is an unstable arrhythmia. At the primary level, the correct response is to stabilize using ABCs and refer immediately to tertiary care. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Administer oral beta-blocker and monitor for 2 hours
Perform a 12-lead ECG and refer the next day
Begin ABC stabilization and urgently refer to tertiary care
Attempt vagal maneuvers and discharge if heart rate improves

steps.title: Supraventricular Tachycardia
steps.text: Which of the following is an appropriate vagal manoeuvre for infants with haemodynamically stable SVT at the secondary care level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: For infants, applying ice or iced water to the face is a safe and effective vagal manoeuvre to attempt termination of SVT. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Asking the infant to hold their breath
Applying an ice pack or iced water to the face for up to 30 seconds
Having the infant blow into a straw

steps.title: Supraventricular Tachycardia
steps.text: What is the correct initial non-pharmacologic intervention for an older child with stable SVT at secondary care? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The Valsalva maneuver is a recommended first-line intervention in older children who are stable with SVT. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Use the Valsalva maneuver (e.g., blowing into a straw)

steps.title: Chest Pain
steps.text: In evaluating pediatric chest pain at a secondary or tertiary facility, which of the following warrants referral to a cardiologist? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Chest pain that is triggered or worsened by activity may suggest an underlying cardiac cause and should be referred to cardiology. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Normal ECG and no family history of cardiac conditions
Chest pain localized to a single rib with reproducible tenderness
Chest pain associated with physical exertion

steps.title: Chest Pain
steps.text: Which of the following findings would most strongly indicate the need for cardiology referral in a child with chest pain? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: ECG abnormalities and a family history of cardiomyopathy, long QT syndrome, or sudden death are clear indications for cardiology referral. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Mild cough and normal chest X-ray
ECG abnormalities or positive family history of cardiomyopathy
Normal cardiovascular examination and no systemic symptoms

steps.title: Syncope
steps.text: A child presents with a brief loss of consciousness but has a normal exam and vitals. What is the most appropriate action at the primary or secondary level? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Syncope may have cardiac or neurological causes. Evaluation by a cardiologist or neurologist at a tertiary center is advised. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Refer to a tertiary facility for cardiology or neurology evaluation
Order a 24-hour EEG before deciding next steps
